speaker: interviewer
text: And I fear once we're done with the interview, we'll tend to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. OK. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we can keep moving through. Yeah. Let me help. These labels.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we have others.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. He didn't send me any of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have them in the boxes. OK. You ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you get the a box down here. Are they doing-- Oh, there's some masterings with digital betacam, aren't there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: For some reason, I was thinking it was going down to Avid or Lightworks.

speaker: interviewer
text: No no no, because we're not editing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's just dubs. Only dubs are made.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the night while we were in the wall-- oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on. We're with Betty Gold in Cleveland on April 16, 1995. This is tape number 3. And we're speeding. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were about to say while you were in the wall. You were going to tell us something about when you were in the wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You forgot it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But you were living in the swamp while your father was preparing. How many people with you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine. And we were like sardines. [INAUDIBLE] afterwards. I'm sorry. Because the bridge was small, just enough. If one person turned over, everybody had to turn over and so forth. We were nine people. And we would go out and, like I said, steal fruit from the orchards and the gardens and live on whatever was available to us from garbage cans. Or, they didn't have garbage cans in those days. But whatever we could find in backyards, like dogs, we would just eat. And then it became more dangerous to go out because we heard a lot of noises from the villages, and there were a lot of shepherds. And we could hear them talking from far, far away, so we were careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they started to send me for food only at night. And I had a little babushka, and I would go out. And I would, you know, find as much food as I could. And in order for me to get back to the swamp, to the bridge-- because we were far in, I wouldn't know how to get back because I would lose the direction. So this was a signal. We clapped. So it could've been animals or birds. And they would clap back to me. And that's how they directed me to come to the bridge, to our homestead, with the food, whether it was a piece of bread or a few apples-- whatever I could gather up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how we lived. That's what we ate. And for water, we used rain. We had one little pot. We used rainwater. We drank rainwater. Whenever possible, we stored it. Or sometimes from the swamp, even, if it got bad. We were so thirsty, and couldn't even have a choice. And you just sat there. You just sat there.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, there were a few Gentile people, too, that my father told about our hiding because we just were so hungry, and didn't have what to wear, what to eat anymore, that in fact, what have we got to lose? We've got to tell our closest Gentile friends-- customers they were, actually-- where we are. Maybe they'll help us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they did. And we-- my mother-- my mother ran away with a few gold pieces and a gold ring. I don't know what she had on her person. And of course, they did it, but they didn't do it for nothing. [INAUDIBLE] We gave them all the gold and all the money, whatever we had, until there was nothing left. And it wasn't that much to begin with. And they did help us out with a little bread. And they would come and bring, drop off, a few packages here and there. And we were extremely grateful. It was just a lifesaver for us. And they did not report us. So they were loyal, and they were righteous people. And to this day, we're very grateful. And we always-- my parents always send them packages and medication to Poland in appreciation and help them in any way they could. At that time they did help us. There were two families. After that, winter set in. And we started to hide in the caves, in the bunkers. And it got to be really very cold. We had a fur coat with us. It wasn't even a fur coat. It was like a coat lined with fur. It was a men's coat, my father's coat. Men wore those in those days. It looked like Persian lamb inside. I don't know what it was. And that was our cover. We treasured it. I mean, that was the only source of heat and whatever clothing we had that didn't fall apart yet. And that's how we lived. And then the winter set in. And it really became disastrous because you couldn't go out for food. The footsteps would lead the, the villagers to us, and they would find us. If you didn't eat for three days, you just didn't eat for three days. You couldn't go out yet. You had to wait for a storm. You had to wait for a snowstorm, or until the snow melted, or otherwise you couldn't survive. And one time, it was a really wonderful happening-- my oldest brother got a hold of seven loaves of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: How? He got them from a house. He stole them from a house, from a Polish home. They were baking bread, and he stole them. I don't remember how he got it. But I remember him coming with the bread. And we almost attacked him, you know? Everybody wanted the bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my dad built a shelf inside the cave. He dug a little shelf out. And he stored the breads there. And he gave us a speech that it's winter. We can't go out for food. Now this bread  is going to have to last as long as possible. Nobody gets more than one piece a day. And he showed us the size. He pointed with his fingers. And we were nine people in the cave. And like I said before, one turned around, everybody had to turn. It's that close. And we could sit up. But we could not stand up. And we were just lying there day and night, looking at each other, hardly talking. And eventually the fur coat got full of lice. And we had to get rid of that because it ate us up. And eventually the lice got into our hair. I had very long pigtails and long hair. And my father had a knife and my cousin had a knife. And they sat down, and they cut my hair off, one by one. This was the most painful thing I ever experienced. And they just took like one strand at a time and just cut it off at the bottom until they cut my hair off, because the only way to get rid of the lice. And it was a very, very sad day in the caves for me that I really stands out. And we were cold, and we were hungry, and we were desperate. And we just-- times were so bad already. We had like a little tiny oven. I don't know how we got it from someone or what. It would make a little fire. And if we found potato peelings, we would-- my brother would lick them like this and I, and we'd stick them outside of the little iron-- the little stove. Maybe it was this big, a little square. And we would cook the potato peelings and eat them, like that. Of course, we got so sick afterwards because they were garbage. We got them out of the garbage, and they were smelly. And they were rotten. And then we would throw it all up anyway. We couldn't handle it. But it filled us up momentarily. So we used to do that. Well, at one point, one of the Gentile people came, and we thought it was somebody found us. They're going to kill us. No. I'm sorry. Yes, one of the Gentile people came. And we thought it was the Germans or the Ukrainians found us. And nobody wanted to go and see who it is. Nobody wanted to go, to look out who it is. We heard the footsteps right out. If they take one more step, they'd fall in because the opening, they reached the opening. And at that point, my, my younger brother was asleep. And they woke him up and they said, go see who's there. He didn't know what it was all about, so he went to see. Oh, he says, it's Jusef, our friend, the Polish person. Jusef came to tell us that he heard in the village that there's a cave of people, Jews, in the woods, in the Radziwill woods, a dozen or so Jews. And they're going to come to get them tonight. And he came to warn us that they found out about us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that moment, we knew of another family from our town who survived the killings, who ran away, that were about two miles away from us in a cave also. So my father set out to visit that family. And he went there to beg them to let us in because Jusef came to tell us that we're going to be killed tonight. And we would freeze if we didn't have a cave. And that was the closest cave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father got there and they said, we can't. There's no room. There wasn't. And there was no room to get in there. And he came back. And he told us the news that there's no room. And he saw for himself there was no room. And we can't run. Enough is enough. Whatever will happen will happen. And they sat us down and they tell us all, look, when they come here to kill us, this is what we're going to do. Don't wait. Get out and run. You'll get hurt at least on the run. You'll get shot. If they find us alive, nine people, they'll cut us to pieces. That's what they used to do. They used to find women and men in the woods, they cut their breasts off, their tongues off, their feet, and hang them on the trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainians, right. So my father and my cousin, they were the leaders of the group, told everyone this is what we're going to do. You get out, and they start shooting, just run. Don't wait for them to hang you or cut you. Just run. Let them shoot you in the back, let them kill you. But don't stand there. And if anybody survives, we'll meet there and there and there. OK. So we lied there and we were waiting. We were waiting for the Ukrainians to come and kill us. And we lied there, and nobody is coming. All of a sudden, we hear shooting, grenades, and shooting and rifles. And we hear those terrible, terrible sounds. What happened, it was the other cave that they discovered. And they killed all the other people. Had they taken us in, if they would have had room for us, we would have been killed with them. And that was our luck. Go figure it out. A miracle that we survived. And we were ready. We were so ready that we all kissed each other goodbye.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the funniest thing is when my mother and father kissed each other goodbye, and my father would say to my mother [NON-ENGLISH]-- I mean, come on-- which means, stay well. You're going to get killed. What do you mean, stay well? You're saying goodbye because you're going to get killed, both of you. So he wishes her to stay well. So we laughed. We really giggled at that moment. If it wouldn't be for a sense of humor, I don't think we'd survive because that's the only thing that kept us going. We laughed at ourselves and cried at ourselves. It was just-- ran out of emotions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they kissed each other goodbye. And we waited and waited. They never came to us. And it was the other people that got killed. After that, it was so bad. And there was so much snow. We couldn't go out. And we stayed there because they didn't find us. But we needed food. We needed food. And then the Polish people couldn't come either because of the snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided, in the meantime, all the people who ran away from the first ghetto, from the first killing, came back to town. And there was another ghetto, again. The Germans kept them working at the leather factory for a while. And we knew already because we met people in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once in awhile, we'd run into somebody in the woods. And we'd spy and see, if it was-- oh, my god, one of ours, one of ours-- Moshe, Haim, or whatever, whoever it was. [INAUDIBLE] And they would tell us that there's a ghetto in town again. And that they run away, blah blah blah. And they went back, and there-- there about 200 or 300 or 400 people in the ghetto now. Well, we were so desperate. We were so hungry and so cold, and we had no clothes at that time anymore. Our feet were wrapped in-- in leaves and in-- in-- I still have frozen toes. My brother had frozen toes. We still suffer from it. And my mother would put my feet between whatever breast [LAUGHS] she had left to warm them up. And my father would blow like this on my hands and my feet to keep us warm. But we got so desperate. And when we found out there was another ghetto and shtetl, we decided that we're gonna go. And whatever happens to everybody else will happen to us. It's enough. We can't-- we can't handle it anymore. No clothes, no food, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get up. And at that time, it was already towards spring. It was already, I remember, getting warm-- maybe April. I don't know exactly the month. And we got up, and we decided we're all going to go to the ghetto to join the rest of the Jewish friends and family, whoever survived. And we--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my father was born in a shtetl. His cousin was born in a shtetl. All of us were born  in a shtetl, the nine people. They knew their way around in the middle of the night. I mean, you just blindfold them, they'd get anywhere. And we started to proceed to go to the ghetto. And it was daylight broke. It was dawn, like. And we started to walk. It was still dark. But by the time we reached the shtetl it was already dawn. We were lost for hours and hours and hours and hours. My father couldn't understand why he can't find his way back to his shtetl. And it wasn't that far. It was a few miles. We walked this way. We went west and east. No, I think it's this way. No, I think it's this way. Again, one of the kids said, why don't you follow your nose. And everybody starts laughing [LAUGHS] in the middle of all this. And it got to a point where were so tired and so exhausted from walking, and we were so [NON-ENGLISH]. We didn't know where we were. So they told us, let's just sit. We ran into a drench. And we said, we'll wait until it gets a little bit light outside, we'll see at least where we are. We could hardly walk. And all of us were so skinny and frail. And dawn was coming and all of a sudden, my father and-- and his cousins and the other people said, oh my god. They were in the backyards, practically. Maybe-- maybe 1,000 or 2,000 feet away from where we-- where our destination was. And we didn't realize it. While we were in the drench, we heard shooting. We heard shots, lot a shots. We didn't know what it was. And as soon as dawn-- daylight broke a little bit, they-- the men went to find people. The went and they climbed into a window to tell the people, can you take us out, the nine of us. We came back to the ghetto. We don't have where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were crawling and crawling and crawling and they got into the window. And they came, and there was nobody in that house. We went into another house. There was nobody in that house.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the rest of the people that night got shot, killed. By again, another miracle, that we got lost. And that's the only reason we remained alive. And there was nobody left. Everybody was killed that night. While we were walking to the shtetl, they were all killed. We started to go. We started all over again. We didn't know where to start. I don't know why we didn't commit suicide. We didn't know how, maybe, or what. But really, nobody wanted to live anymore. We didn't know what to do. And I don't know. You just do what your heart tells you, or instinct, or whatever. Of course, as children, we did what our parents told us. We turned around. We didn't have any strength anymore. We waited for night time. We turned around. And we went to another cave. And we went there and we have absolutely nothing to eat. And it was already three days. I mean, we were starving, literally. Our tongues were hanging out. We were pale. We were-- it's a miracle we didn't eat each other. Went outside, we took some snow, and that's what kept us alive. It was some snow, a little bit of rain, and a few leaves. We picked any leaves we could find we thought maybe won't hurt us. And we ate them, and we threw up. But we ate. That's what we ate is leaves. I got my period. I was exactly 12 years old-- no, 11 years old, already. I got my period. I didn't know what it was all about. I'm skipping. That was a bit later. I'm sorry. That was really towards the end. That was a bit later. But anyway, we were still in the woods and I got my period. And I didn't know. I knew a little bit. But I didn't know what it was all about. I was lying next to my daddy, how close we were next to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the cave or outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cave. And I woke up. And I was such a bloody mess from here to here. My father was a bloody mess, from here to here. I didn't know if people was bleeding. I didn't know if I was bleeding. I didn't know where it came from. But somebody killed him, or somebody choked him, or somebody-- I don't know what happened. I got hysterical. And I started screaming and crying. Well, my mother took me out with a lady cousin, my cousin. And they took me under a tree. And there was a puddle of water, and they washed me up. And they told me about the birds and the bees.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my remark was even you know, they explained to me what that's and the what-- what difference did it make? I mean, I had no social life in there. [LAUGHS] There was nobody to tell. I couldn't get pregnant or anything. But they started to warn me what could happen and so forth and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my question to them was, oh, you mean, I could have a baby, even if my brother did it. I was-- not that was a place and a time to explain to a child. We had more important things [LAUGHS] to worry about. Why wasn't I prepared. I was very angry. I was very angry. And it was-- it was really a very sad moment. And I was very embarrassed. I was so humiliated when I found out that it was me causing my dad to look like that, and everybody else looking at me and staring at me, and even teasing me. You know, my brother and another boy, same age, they were teasing me, laughing at me. And I was crying. I was so angry. I was angry why it had to happen there. I was angry why I wasn't told. I was angry. I was just angry. Why-- why is it coming to me. What do I have. What kind of life is it. What do I need it for. Why didn't we go to the ghetto and get killed with the rest of our family. And I was just so sad. I wanted to kill myself. I really wanted to kill myself. And of course, they protected me. My parents protected me. And again, they save-- you know, they saved me. And there was another sad incident in the woods. There was a cave not far from us. And there was a mother-- not a mother. I'm sorry. There was a father-- a landsman-- a father, a child, and a few other people. The little girl couldn't be more than three or four-- three years old. And the little girl not very, very sick, and she died. And we had to leave-- they had to leave their cave because somebody spotted 'em. And they spotted us. So we all left our caves and we left the little girl under a trash of leaves. We covered her with leaves. And we figured maybe next night, when it quiets down, in the dark we'll go and bury her. Well, we all came back to bury her. And she was breathing. And my mother put her in her bosom. And my father was breathing in her mouth. And her father was-- I mean, it was a scene like, [SIGHS] not even in the movies, like in-- in the jungle, an animal jungle. It was a zoo, like the animals killing animals. It was just atrocious to watch a child dying. You know, they brought her back to life. I don't-- don't ask me how. We had no food. Maybe a little water from someplace. Or maybe she sucked another piece of food that we found or some. We brought that little girl back to life. My mother and my cousin and all the other adults brought her back to life. They would warm her and keep her next to their body under their clothes. And they would breathe in her mouth little by little, and they would give her drops in her mouth. And they would-- I mean, we all took such care of this little baby. She was our most prized possession. [LAUGHS] Nobody cared about anybody. They-- they would spit and give her the, you know, in her mouth to give her a little fluid. Everybody contributed. It was so sad. And I was a little kid myself. But I just-- I just couldn't watch anymore. And we always covered her because we were cold. And we took turns to hold her and pet her. Anyway, this little [LAUGHS] girl [INAUDIBLE] survived the war. She really survived with her father, out of seven people in the family. And unfortunately, she died a few years ago of cancer. But she really had a wonderful, wonderful life after the war. She was like a princess. She married a Prince Charming, had marvelous five kids and a wonderful family and a lot of joy and pleasure. And to this day, her kids are like-- I'm like a-- like a mother to them. They are always keeping in touch with me. They always visit me. And I'm always there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this woman's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Rita Katz. And she eventually came to the United States. After the war she married-- her father got married to a lady in the DP camps. And she became her mother because she was only about at that time seven years old. And then they immigrated to the United States in Columbus to their family. And she had a wonderful life afterwards. And she left a wonderful, wonderful family behind. And her name is really being carried on in the most wonderful Jewish way, traditional way and all professional kids and lovely children.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we want to talk about you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And so that little girl, we saved that little girl. And again, it got to a point where it was hard to survive. But it-- from a standpoint, it was cold and no food. But spring came. The warm weather came again. And we transferred to different woods, a different part, because we were afraid that too many-- with spring coming, the shepherds or other people from the village would spot us. And we heard too many noises there, and we were afraid. So we went to our cave about three, four miles away. We transferred. And then we came out doors a little bit as soon as the leaves came out, and we could be sheltered. And we built a little fire one time, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already 1944, summer 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a question. During this whole period, though, I mean, I realize you were living in caves. You were living in the caves, and were there Germans everywhere? How-- what kind of-- I mean, because you've-- I realize that you can't tell me every single day, but in terms of the Germans, did you have-- what were you aware of? Were you aware of what was going on in Treblinka or in Auschwitz? Were you hearing things? Did you know anything at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We didn't know what's going on. We didn't know anything. We didn't even know the war is over. Sometimes, not until my father would see a Polish person or somebody who would tell us. But otherwise, we didn't know because there was no communication. There was nobody to talk to. Eventually, the partisans found us. But otherwise, we wouldn't have known when the war was over. May be in time, I suppose, when we go out to steal food and bump into somebody or whatever. Otherwise, we had no idea what's going on in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, would it be safe to say that from approximately like that summer after you went in-- after that-- after the Germans came and took that first cave with those other people, from that point on, were you living in-- was it kind of total isolation? Was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, absolutely. Except to venture out for food, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these nine people, who were the other-- can you just tell me who they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, my brother, a neighbor's child, who was two years older than I am-- who survived. And two other friends of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And both your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother. My older brother went to take care of cousins of mine in a different grave-- in a different cave, part of the woods, far away from us, because they were just girls-- cousins-- just girls. And they needed somebody else. And he volunteered to go and take care of them and stay with them. And we were separated at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in these underground caves, were you able to make any kind of fire so you could cook anything? Or even if it was just taking leaves with water and making a soup. I mean, what did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe-- maybe once a month, once or two months-- very, very seldom, very seldom. Mostly maybe in the summertime a little bit outdoors a real tiny little fire because we were afraid of the smoke. And were, you know-- You couldn't do that. But sometimes in the summer, when the leaves were in full bloom and it was noisy and the birds would and nobody could hear us, we would be far in the woods, we'd build our little fire, yes. We would do that occasionally, but very, very seldom, very seldom. We had a little stove that we carried with us. It was just-- like I said before, we didn't have what to cook anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So oh, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and believe or not, that after that, there were still a few people went back to, to the shtetl. There was still the Ukrainians and a few the Nazis running it. And of course, at the end they all got killed. We did not go back anymore. We did not go back again.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the woods until the summer of 1944, if I remember correctly. And my brother ventured out. We were close by next to an orchard like. And the green apples were just beginning to come in. They weren't ripe or anything, little ones. And he went out to get some of those. And we were standing there. We were outside the cave because, like I said, it was-- the trees were sheltering us. And we were looking and waiting for him to come back. And all of a sudden, we see horses. And one of the horses, there's a soldier on the horse. And another horse was someone in civilian uniform, but they had a rifle on them. And the third horse was another soldier with a rifle. And one of them was holding my brother. They caught him stealing the apples. And they asked him to lead us where he came from. He was a little boy. And he told them. And he led them to our cave.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop here to change the tape.
